items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.q: Why is interspecific competition between similar species important?
items.correct: Two species cannot occupy exactly the same niche indefinitely — competition often leads to displacement or niche divergence.
items.misconception: Competition only happens between predators and their prey.
items.random_wrong: Competition means physically fighting and is rare in nature.
items.why: Students associate 'competition' with literal fighting, missing the ecological meaning of competing for shared resources.

items.q: How do decomposers return carbon to the atmosphere?
items.correct: They respire — breaking down dead organic matter and releasing CO2.
items.misconception: Decomposers release carbon by physically breaking matter into smaller pieces.
items.random_wrong: Decomposers turn dead matter into oxygen and water, with no carbon involved.
items.why: Students see decomposition as a physical breakdown rather than a metabolic respiration process happening in microbes and fungi.

items.q: Which term refers to all populations of different species living together in one area?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Community. “Population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Soil pH. “Predation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An ecosystem contains 200 rabbits, 50 foxes and 20 oak trees. How many populations are present (assuming each species counts as one)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
